team_information.team_challenge: In Motion: Technical Challenge – Engineering a Kinetic Story

appraiser_information.appraisers: Jordan Lee (Head Appraiser, Team Challenge); Priya Desai (Structure & Engineering Appraiser); Michael Alvarez (Performance Appraiser)

reason_for_nomination.reason_for_nomination:
We, the undersigned appraisers for the "In Motion" Technical Team Challenge at the Mid-State Regional Tournament, are nominating Team 145-32789, the Quantum Kinetic Crew (Middle Level), for the Renaissance Award in recognition of their exceptional engineering execution, meticulous design, and highly polished integration of technical elements into their performance.

From the moment their solution was set up, it was clear this team operated with a level of engineering discipline and craftsmanship that is uncommon even at higher levels. Their central device was a multi-stage, chain-reaction kinetic system built around a custom-designed, gear-driven transfer tower. The tower itself was constructed primarily from laser-cut plywood and 3D-printed brackets, all of which they designed themselves using free CAD software (they specifically mentioned using Tinkercad and Fusion 360 at school). The tolerances on the interlocking gear sets were notably tight; there was no visible wobble or misalignment, and the meshing was smooth enough that the system ran quietly and consistently over multiple test runs and the official performance.

One of the most impressive aspects of their design was the way they managed energy transfer between stages. Instead of relying on a single type of motion, they intentionally combined rotational, linear, and gravitational potential energy in a carefully sequenced series:

1. A hand-cranked flywheel stored rotational energy, which was then released via a magnetic latch they engineered using repelling magnets and a sliding collar. This latch consistently triggered within a very narrow angle of rotation, demonstrating thoughtful prototyping and refinement.

2. The released flywheel drove a vertical gear train that lifted a weighted cart up a track. The track included side rails and a simple but effective anti-rollback ratchet system made from cut acrylic and small dowel pins. The ratchet teeth were hand-cut but evenly spaced, and the pawl movement was damped by a small rubber band to reduce bounce—an unusually refined touch for this level.

3. At the top of the track, the cart engaged a lever arm that released a series of precision-timed marbles. These marbles traveled through a custom-built chute network with carefully calculated slopes. The team explained that they had experimented with different angles to avoid the marbles either stalling or gaining too much speed and jumping the tracks. The final design demonstrated consistent, predictable behavior.

4. The marbles ultimately triggered a weighted pendulum that swung through an arc and struck a hinged plate. The plate, in turn, completed an electrical circuit that illuminated a set of LED indicators embedded into their backdrop. The team had wired the circuit themselves, using a breadboard, resistors sized appropriately for the LEDs, and a 9V battery. Their wiring was neat, labeled, and bundled—more in line with what we would expect from a high school robotics team than a typical middle level DI team.

The structural integrity and reliability of the device were also noteworthy. During prep and performance, the team conducted quick pre-run checks, tightening set screws on gears, verifying alignment of the cart rails, and checking continuity on their LED circuit. There was no visible tape-as-a-fix or last-minute patchwork; everything appeared to have been designed with maintainability in mind. The entire device broke down into three main modules that could be transported separately and then reassembled with alignment marks and color-coded connectors.

In terms of design aesthetics, the team went beyond mere functionality. They intentionally integrated their engineering elements into the story world. The gear tower was themed as a "Chrono Engine" inside a fictional time-keeper’s workshop, and all visible structural pieces were painted in a cohesive palette of aged brass, deep blue, and dark wood tones. They used dry-brushing techniques to give 3D-printed plastic pieces the appearance of metal, and they sanded and stained the wooden base to match. Even their fasteners were chosen and painted to blend into the theme rather than distract from it.

What distinguishes this team for the Renaissance Award is not just that they built something that worked, but that they clearly followed a rigorous engineering process. When interviewed informally after the performance, the team members described how they:

- Built and tested three different versions of the gear ratio between the crank and the lift mechanism to find a balance between speed and control.
- Logged failure modes in a notebook (they showed us pages of sketches and test notes) and systematically addressed each problem, such as marbles derailing, gears slipping on axles, and early wear on cardboard prototypes.
- Used simple measurement tools (a school protractor and a smartphone slow-motion camera) to fine-tune the pendulum swing so it would reliably strike the trigger plate with enough force to close the circuit without damaging it.

Their performance discipline matched their engineering. The student operating the crank remained in character as an apprentice time-keeper, narrating the sequence in a way that highlighted the engineering without breaking the story. They timed their dialogue to the mechanical events, so each stage of the device’s operation corresponded to a specific narrative beat. This indicated extensive rehearsal and a strong understanding of how to integrate technical and theatrical elements.

Moreover, the team’s safety practices were exemplary. They had clearly thought through pinch points and potential hazards. Moving parts were either shielded or placed out of reach of the audience, and they had a clearly defined role for one team member as a designated safety checker before starting the device. They even had a small laminated checklist taped to the side of the main structure, which they quickly ran through before the official run began.

We also want to highlight the precision and consistency of their solution. During practice runs in the challenge room (before scoring began), the device operated flawlessly multiple times in a row. In the official scored performance, every stage triggered exactly as intended, with no visible improvisation needed to compensate for a failure. This degree of reliability at a tournament—where nerves and minor environmental changes often cause issues—is strong evidence of robust engineering and thorough testing.

Finally, while this award is specifically for skill rather than creativity, it is worth noting that the team’s disciplined approach to engineering actually enabled their creativity to shine. Because they were confident in their mechanisms, they were able to focus on timing, characterization, and storytelling instead of worrying about whether things would work. The end result was a polished, professional-feeling solution where the engineering was not just a requirement to be met, but a central, celebrated feature of the performance.

For all of these reasons—precision in construction, thoughtful energy transfer design, reliable and repeatable operation, integration of electrical and mechanical systems, careful attention to safety and maintainability, and a sophisticated understanding of how to weave technical skill into a narrative—we believe the Quantum Kinetic Crew exemplifies the purpose of the Renaissance Award for outstanding design and engineering. We strongly and enthusiastically recommend Team 145-32789 for this honor.
